topic: Economic Objectives and the Role of Government
subject: economics

items.q: How is economic growth shown on a Production Possibility Curve?
items.correct: By an outward shift of the entire curve — reflecting an increase in productive capacity through more or better resources, technology, or skills.
items.misconception: Growth is shown by moving from inside the curve to a point on the curve.
items.random_wrong: Growth is shown by the curve becoming straighter.
items.why: Moving from inside to on the curve is RECOVERY (using existing resources better), not growth. True growth requires the curve to shift outward — students confuse recovery from inefficiency with new capacity.

items.correct: The difference between what consumers are willing to pay and what they actually pay — represented by the area below the demand curve and above the price line.
items.misconception: Consumer surplus is leftover stock that consumers haven't bought yet.
items.random_wrong: Consumer surplus is the profit that consumers make.
items.why: 'Surplus' is misleadingly physical-sounding. It is a welfare measure — the value consumers get beyond what they paid. Students take the everyday meaning of 'surplus' and miss the economic concept.

items.q: What is asymmetric information as a source of market failure?
items.correct: When one party in a transaction knows more than the other — leading to problems like adverse selection (used-car market) or moral hazard (insurance). Markets can fail or unravel when information is unequal.
items.misconception: Asymmetric information means a buyer and seller disagree about a price.
items.random_wrong: Asymmetric information is when a market only operates in one direction.
items.why: Disagreement about value is normal trade. Asymmetric information is about one side genuinely knowing more — Akerlof's 'lemons' problem shows how this can destroy entire markets. Students collapse the concept into ordinary haggling.

items.q: Changes to interest rates in the UK are decided by:
items.correct: MPC of the Bank of England
items.why: The answer examiners want is: MPC of the Bank of England. “HM Treasury” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which is a fiscal policy tool?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Income tax. “Interest rates” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many members does the Bank of England MPC have?
items.why: Exact answer: 9 members. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
